relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Eliphaz begins his final speech.
He questions whether human righteousness benefits the Divine.
He says Shaddai gains nothing from Job’s wholeness.
He denies that Job is being judged for reverence.
He directly accuses Job of great evil.
He imagines Job exploiting brothers and the vulnerable.
He accuses Job of withholding water and bread.
He pictures power taking the land.
He accuses Job of sending widows away and crushing orphans.
Therefore, he says, snares and dread surround Job.
Darkness and flood cover him.
Eliphaz points to the high heavens and stars.
He accuses Job of thinking God cannot see through clouds.
Clouds become, in his accusation, a hiding place from judgment.
He warns Job against the ancient path of the wicked.
He says they were swept away before their time.
They told Shaddai to depart.
Yet their houses had been filled with good things.
The righteous rejoice over their fall in Eliphaz’s picture.
Fire consumes what remains.
Eliphaz calls Job to make peace with the Divine.
He tells him to receive instruction into the heart.
If Job returns to Shaddai, Eliphaz promises rebuilding.
Gold must become dust compared with the Divine.
Shaddai would become treasure.
Then Job’s face would lift.
Prayer would be heard and vows fulfilled.
Light would shine on Job’s ways.
The lowly would be lifted.
Deliverance would come through clean hands.
